channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - kotaku.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for kotaku.com

item.title: A Fan Claims To Have Found A Set From Alex Garland’s Elden Ring Movie Out In The Wild
item.content:encoded:
Footage from the set of Alex Garland’s upcoming Elden Ring movie has made its way online, after a content creator happened to spot the set just somewhere in a random English field. While the legitimacy of the footage has been called into question, which is fair enough in the age of AI-generated slop, Kotaku has been able to speak to the original uploader of the clip and track down another video of the set.

Announced back in May 2025, the Elden Ring movie was approved by Hidetaka Miyazaki himself, after director Alex Garland flew to Japan and presented him with a 160-page draft of his vision. Since then, we’ve not actually heard too much about the project, beyond a few casting announcements (including Ben Whishaw and Cailee Spaeny, who have been cast in undisclosed roles).

While it was previously stated that production company A24 intended to start filming in 2026, there was no exact announcement regarding the start of the film’s principal photography. However, based on the video provided by Elden Ring streamer THROX on TikTok, we can probably expect an announcement on that front relatively soon.

Kotaku reached out to THROX to ask them a few questions about their discovery, and they confirmed that the set they stumbled upon is somewhere in England. THROX also stated that they didn’t simply discover it by chance, as a friend of his was actually the first to spot the set and passed the information on to them.

THROX also had one more picture and another video of the set on their Discord, and stated that they took several more videos of different locations on the set, which they intend to upload at a later date, including one that features “some barrels and some like wooden carriages from the game.” THROX has since uploaded one of these new videos, embedded below.

I’ve done a little digging to see if I could line up the Church of Marika in THROX’s video with a specific Church of Marika in Elden Ring, and none of them line up exactly. However, the Elden Ring film has been rumored to be a prequel of sorts, so with that in mind, I think it might end up being the Second Church of Marika, but in a slightly less dilapidated state.

Or, hey, it could be a fifth Church of Marika that was never featured in the game. Either way, I think the area itself in THROX’s videos looks a lot like Limgrave. Smart of the production company to save on VFX costs by shooting the film in England, because now they won’t have to alter the weather in post to make things look dark and miserable.



https://kotaku.com/elden-ring-moavie-alex-garland-filming-set-2000684895
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Ex-Nintendo Sales Lead Explains The Three Factors Making A Switch 2 Price Hike ‘Inevitable’
item.content:encoded:
Nintendo is the last holdout among the big three when it comes to raising the price of its current-gen flagship gaming console. How much longer can it keep the Switch 2 at the launch MSRP of $450? A former Nintendo sales lead thinks an upcoming price hike is inevitable, and Trump’s war on Iran might only be speeding things up.

“Unfortunately, I think, eventually the hardware price is going to have to go up,” the ex-employee, who goes only by “Sean” to protect his anonymity, told fellow Nintendo alumni Kit Ellis and Krysta Yang on a recent episode of their podcast. “I think that there’s things that they can and seem to be doing to try and mitigate that, but I also look at this move on software as, if I’m reading it correctly, a way to make a hardware price increase a little bit more palatable,” he said, referring to the recent announcement that soon digital versions of Switch 2 games will be cheaper than their physical counterparts.

He pointed to this as one way Nintendo may be trying to sweeten the deal for what will otherwise be a more expensive console generation for everyone involved. The main drivers of the pressure to raise prices continue to be tariffs, which Nintendo is suing the Trump government over, as well as the AI-fueled shortage of RAM and other PC components.

“We’ve seen inflation being a problem for a while now,” Sean continued. “Tariffs are a more recent nuisance, but they’re not going away anytime soon. The demand that AI is causing for chips is causing memory prices to go up.” But he added that the war on Iran is also not helping. The problem isn’t just rising oil prices, which affects the cost of transporting goods, but also disruption of resources needed for manufacturing parts.

“Helium is a byproduct of of producing oil. Helium is a key and unreplaceable ingredient in making semiconductors, which means hardware prices go up,” he said. “It’s an unreplaceable byproduct of making silicon wafers, which means if you’re Nintendo and you’re producing cartridges, that’s going up as well.”

Nintendo can try to absorb some of the pressure through its other revenue streams, from toys and licensing deals to movies and theme parks, but Sean argues that there are just too many economic factors moving against it.

“I think it’s inevitable that they’re going to go up for the first time,” Sean said. “And, you know, we’ve been through various phases with Nintendo through various economic turns and things, but it does really feel like this time in particular, there’s just so many outside forces that [are] kind of forcing their hand in a way that they probably aren’t really used to in the past.”



https://kotaku.com/switch-2-price-ram-iran-oil-trump-tariffs-2000684755
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
